wraps)
MethodType)
namedtuple)
TestCase
paramz
args kwargsc
 Represents a single parameter to a test case.
        For example::
            >>> p = param("foo", bar=16)
            >>> p
            param("foo", bar=16)
            >>> p.args
            ('foo', )
            >>> p.kwargs
            {'bar': 16}
        Intended to be used as an argument to ``@parameterized``::
            @parameterized([
                param("foo", bar=16),
            ])
            def test_stuff(foo, bar=16):
                pass
        c
_param
__new__
args
kwargs
J/usr/lib64/python3.9/site-packages/numpy/testing/_private/parameterized.pyr	
param.__new__Nc
 Creates a ``param`` by explicitly specifying ``args`` and
            ``kwargs``::
                >>> param.explicit([1,2,3])
                param(*(1, 2, 3))
                >>> param.explicit(kwargs={"foo": 42})
                param(*(), **{"foo": "42"})
            r
explicitE
param.explicitc
 Returns an instance of ``param()`` for ``@parameterized`` argument
            ``args``::
                >>> param.from_decorator((42, ))
                param(args=(42, ), kwargs={})
                >>> param.from_decorator("foo")
                param(args=("foo", ), kwargs={})
            z
after * must bez=Parameters must be tuples, but %r is not (hint: use '(%r, )')N)
isinstancer
	TypeError)
from_decoratorS
param.from_decoratorc
param(*%r, **%r)r
selfr
__repr__k
param.__repr__)
NN)	
__name__
__module__
__qualname__
__doc__r	
classmethodr
Return tuples of parameterized arguments and their values.
        This is useful if you are writing your own doc_func
        function and need to know the values for each parameter name::
            >>> def func(a, foo=None, bar=42, **kwargs): pass
            >>> p = param(1, foo=7, extra=99)
            >>> parameterized_argument_value_pairs(func, p)
            [("a", 1), ("foo", 7), ("bar", 42), ("**kwargs", {"extra": 99})]
        If the function's first argument is named ``self`` then it will be
        ignored::
            >>> def func(self, a): pass
            >>> p = param(1)
            >>> parameterized_argument_value_pairs(func, p)
            [("a", 1)]
        Additionally, empty ``*args`` or ``**kwargs`` will be ignored::
            >>> def func(foo, *args): pass
            >>> p = param(1)
            >>> parameterized_argument_value_pairs(func, p)
            [("foo", 1)]
            >>> p = param(1, 16)
            >>> parameterized_argument_value_pairs(func, p)
            [("foo", 1), ("*args", (16, ))]
    N
get)
name
default)

	<setcomp>
z5parameterized_argument_value_pairs.<locals>.<setcomp>c

"parameterized_argument_value_pairso
 A shortened repr of ``x`` which is guaranteed to be ``unicode``::
            >>> short_repr("foo")
            u"foo"
            >>> short_repr("123456789", n=4)
            u"12...89"
    z

Nz,Invalid test runner: %r (must be one of: %s)rG
_test_runnersr
_test_runner_override)
set_test_runner
qlq$d
 Guess which test runner we're using by traversing the stack and looking
        for the first matching module. This *should* be reasonably safe, as
        it's done during test discovery where the test runner should be the
        stack frame immediately outside. NFr
_test_runner_guessr)
stack
reversed
	f_globalsr
_test_runner_aliasesrY
record
frame
moduler
detect_runner
 Parameterize a test case::
            class TestInt:
                @parameterized([
                    ("A", 10),
                    ("F", 15),
                    param("10", 42, base=42)
                ])
                def test_int(self, input, expected, base=16):
                    actual = int(input, base=base)
                    assert_equal(actual, expected)
            @parameterized([
                (2, 3, 5)
                (3, 5, 8),
            ])
            def test_add(a, b, expected):
                assert_equal(a + b, expected)
        Nc
input_as_callable
	get_inputrR

z@parameterized.assert_not_in_testcase_subclass.<locals>.<genexpr>zqWarning: '@parameterized' tests won't work inside subclasses of 'TestCase' - use '@parameterized.expand' instead.)

	Exception)
parent_classesr
z-parameterized.assert_not_in_testcase_subclassc
 Returns the list of parent classes of the class currently being defined.
            Will likely only work if called from the ``parameterized`` decorator.
            This function is entirely @brandon_rhodes's fault, as he suggested
            the implementation: http://stackoverflow.com/a/8793684/71522
            
class

f_locals)

z2parameterized._terrible_magic_get_defining_classesc
check_input_valuesr
z1parameterized.input_as_callable.<locals>.<lambda>c
input_valuesr
callabler
parameterized.input_as_callablec
z4parameterized.check_input_values.<locals>.<listcomp>)
z parameterized.check_input_valuesc
 A "brute force" method of parameterizing test cases. Creates new
            test cases and injects them into the namespace that the wrapped
            function is being defined in. Useful for parameterizing tests in
            subclasses of 'UnitTest', where Nose test generators don't work.
            >>> @parameterized.expand([("foo", 1, 2)])
            ... def test_add1(name, input, expected):
            ...     actual = add1(input)
            ...     assert_equal(actual, expected)
            ...
            >>> locals()
            ... 'test_add1_foo_0': <function ...> ...
            >>>
            Z
testcase_func_namez1testcase_func_name= is deprecated; use name_func=r:
stacklevelZ
testcase_func_docz/testcase_func_doc= is deprecated; use doc_func=Nc
param_as_standalone_funcr

z:parameterized.expand.<locals>.parameterized_expand_wrapper)

warn

z?parameterized.param_as_standalone_func.<locals>.standalone_func)

z&parameterized.param_as_standalone_funcc
